S02.meta: // SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.
blocks: · Convert a successful one-off prompt into a reusable prompt card with all the components a colleague (or your future self
· Organize a personal or team prompt library that is easy to search and update
· Recognize when a prompt is going stale and knows what to do about it
· Apply a maintenance discipline so the library grows more useful rather than more cluttered
data.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: WHY PROMPTS GO STALE.
blocks: The task drifts.
The model changes.
The prompt was never generalized.

S05.meta: // SLIDE 05 — BEFORE AND AFTER: PROMOTING A

data.title: BEFORE AND AFTER: PROMOTING A ONE-OFF PROMPT
blocks: · Key decisions (bulleted list, 3–5 items)
Action items (table Action | Owner | Due Date)
· Maximum 300 words
If a due date was not stated in the notes, write "due date not stated" do not estimate
· Do not include decisions or actions not present in the notes

data.title: LIBRARY ORGANIZATION
blocks: · A naming convention. Use a short prefix that indicates the domain (MEET, WRITE, DATA, RESEARCH, CODE) followed by a numb
· An index. A single file that lists every card by ID and purpose. When you are looking for a prompt, you search the index
· A tested flag. Each card should note when it was last tested and whether it is currently working. A card that has not be

data.title: THE HUMAN GATE.
blocks: A prompt library is not a vending machine. Every card in the library was validated by a human who checked the output, no
The Human Gate — The discipline of building a library is itself a quality check. When you try to write the review criteria field and find

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES
blocks: Saving prompts without testing them as templates.
Skipping the review criteria field.
Building a library but never pruning it.
Not versioning.
Library without an index.
